items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

items.q: How do you say 'I am watching TV right now' emphasising the action is ongoing?
items.correct: Estoy viendo la televisión ahora mismo.
items.why: Students use simple present. For ONGOING actions in progress, use ESTAR + GERUNDIO (-ando/-iendo): estoy comiendo, estoy estudiando, estoy hablando. Note: estar, not ser. Useful for describing current activities in tech/free time topics.

items.correct: Could be his/her/your(formal)/their brother - context determines which.
items.why: Students assume su = his. SU is one of Spanish's most context-dependent words. Means his, her, your (usted/ustedes), or their. Speakers often clarify with 'de él', 'de ella', 'de usted' if ambiguous: 'el hermano de él'.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I never use Twitter'?
items.why: It's Nunca uso Twitter.. “Nunca yo uso Twitter.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: to download. “to delete” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many subject-verb endings does Spanish typically have per tense (one per person)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 endings. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
